summary: Gruber reviews the iPhone 8 and 8 Plus, arguing they are far more than half-hearted updates despite being overshadowed by the iPhone X announcement. He praises the glass backs for their improved grip and durability over the jet black aluminum, highlights the A11 Bionic chip as a massive leap, and notes that camera improvements are increasingly driven by computational processing rather than optics. He appreciates True Tone displays and Qi charging while criticizing Apple for still shipping a 5-watt charger and no USB-C cable. His conclusion: these are excellent phones that share most of the iPhone X's internals, but nobody is excited about them because of the X.
key_points: The glass backs represent a meaningful improvement in grip and durability over aluminum, making the Plus size genuinely usable for the first time
The A11 Bionic chip is shared with the iPhone X and represents far more than an incremental improvement, powering new computational photography features
Phone camera improvements are now driven more by computing hardware and software than by advances in lens optics or sensors
True Tone is a feature you only appreciate by noticing its absence on other devices
Apple's continued inclusion of a 5-watt charger and USB-A cable while selling USB-C-only laptops is embarrassing nickel-and-diming
money_quotes: These phones are in no way shape or form some sort of half-hearted or minor update over the iPhone 7.
The specs aren't what matters — the effects are what matters. But the specs are what we can measure, and the faster the chips are, the better the effects are in the user experience.
The real action is in hardware and software.

I think it must be so weird to be a hardware designer at Apple, working to make these devices that look beautiful, knowing that 90+ percent of the people who buy them will put them in a case within minutes of unboxing them and never take the case off.
True Tone, though, is the sort of feature that you don't notice, but rather that you notice the absence of in other devices. It ruins you.
If they hadn't debuted alongside the iPhone X we'd be arguing about whether these are the most impressive new iPhone models since the iPhone 6.
I'll go so far as to call the rinky-dink 5-watt charger the new 16 GB storage tier — a nickel-and-dime move whose time was up a few years ago.
Pretty good for a boring update.
key_insight: The iPhone 8 shares nearly all of the iPhone X's internal advances — A11 chip, computational photography, True Tone, Qi charging — making it an excellent phone unfairly overshadowed by its flashier sibling.
